coredata.dc:title: Europium-based aminoclay containing carbon dots: A new visual fluorescence platform for visual point-of-care testing of tetracycline in various real samples

coredata.dc:description: It is of vital significance to achieve accurate real-time detection of tetracycline (TC). In this research, the ratio fluorescence detection of tetracycline is realized by introducing europium-based aminoclay containing carbon dots with good sensitivity to tetracycline through subsequent surface modification. With the increase of tetracycline concentration, the fluorescence intensity of rare earth europium fluorophore increases continuously, while the fluorescence intensity of CDs is basically unchanged. The CIE chromaticity coordinate confirms that the luminous color of the probe can be adjusted continuously from blue to red. The results exhibit this approach owns the favourable linearity to TC within 0–12.5 μM and 10–25 μM, and the high sensitivity with a low detection limit of 3.133 nM. Moreover, it can entirely satisfy the requirements of field detection because the rapid monitoring time as short as 30 s. The multicolor fluorescence detection strategy is reliable and feasible in the field test of TC in various actual samples. In addition, a TC floating detector based on vertical float structure is designed to achieve the rapid visual detection of TC in simulated flow environment. The superiority of the sensing platform opens up a neo-pathway for point-of-care monitoring of TC residues.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: It is of vital significance to achieve accurate real-time detection of tetracycline (TC). In this research, the ratio fluorescence detection of tetracycline is realized by introducing europium-based aminoclay containing carbon dots with good sensitivity to tetracycline through subsequent surface modification. With the increase of tetracycline concentration, the fluorescence intensity of rare earth europium fluorophore increases continuously, while the fluorescence intensity of CDs is basically unchanged. The CIE chromaticity coordinate confirms that the luminous color of the probe can be adjusted continuously from blue to red. The results exhibit this approach owns the favourable linearity to TC within 0–12.5 μM and 10–25 μM, and the high sensitivity with a low detection limit of 3.133 nM. Moreover, it can entirely satisfy the requirements of field detection because the rapid monitoring time as short as 30 s. The multicolor fluorescence detection strategy is reliable and feasible in the field test of TC in various actual samples. In addition, a TC floating detector based on vertical float structure is designed to achieve the rapid visual detection of TC in simulated flow environment. The superiority of the sensing platform opens up a neo-pathway for point-of-care monitoring of TC residues.

The aminoclay clay and carbon dots integrated visual fluorescence platform indicated point-of-care testing detection of tetracycline.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Tetracycline (TC), a broad-spectrum antibiotic with antibacterial and anti-inflammatory properties, has been extensively used in the feed additives and treatment of infection [1]. However, tetracycline is hard-to-degrade naturally and may accumulate in soil, water, meat, eggs, milk and other foods [2,3]. The residue caused by its excessive use not only causes grievous water environment pollution, and threatens the ecological environment and food security [4,5], but also lead to the emergence of drug-resistant bacteria [6,7], which makes the situation of public health safety increasingly serious [8,9]. In order to protect consumers from these antibiotic residues, many countries have already restricted the maximum residue limits of TC in their products [10–12]. Therefore, it is very important to detect the concentration of TC in the environmental monitoring system. While for household use, visual inspection is more convenient. Compared with the traditional detection methods (liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry [13], high performance liquid chromatography [14] and capillary electrophoresis [15]) limit their application in on-site rapid detection due to expensive instruments, cumbersome detection and time-consuming, while enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay [16] and electrochemical method [17] developed in recent years have the disadvantages of expensive reagents and poor selectivity. Therefore, developing more convenient and effective antibiotic detection methods is the goal of researchers. Up to now, various fluorescence detection methods [18–20] have attracted high-profile because of its simple operation, strong specificity and easy to realize visual detection [21]. Particularly, the ratio fluorescence strategy contains a built-in self-calibration system that can minimize the interference of single fluorescence intensity changes, instrument errors or environmental factors, so as to achieve more sensitive and precise analysis [22,23], and more sensitive fluorescence color change, which is exactly what visual quantitative detection needs [24,25]. Although some sensing platforms based on ratio fluorescence detection of TC have been developed, there are few studies on the combination of aminoclay (AC), carbon nanodots and rare earth ions for visual trace detection of TC.

As a novel luminescent material with simple preparation, low cost and environmental friendliness [26], carbon dots (CDs) have made remarkable applications in the fields of chemical detection [27,28], biosensor [29,30] and optoelectronic devices [31]. It is noteworthy that the application of CDs in tetracycline detection mostly depends on the change of monochromatic fluorescence signal, but this polyaromatic ring structure of tetracycline has a relatively weak fluorescence quenching effect on some carbon dots, which may lead to false-positive or false-negative results [32]. Nevertheless, ratio fluorescence probes constructed by using carbon dots as internal parameters can eliminate this effect and make the results more accurate and reliable. Meanwhile, the rare earth ions (for instance, Eu3+) can chelate with the β-diketone configuration of TC and obtain the effective energy of TC via the “antenna effect” to sensitize the luminescence of europium ions [33]. The resulted Eu-TC complex possesses a fluorescence lifetime, which can effectively reduce background interference and scattered fluorescence [34]. In addition, the self-luminous efficiency of Eu3+ is relatively weak due to fluorescence quenching caused by the OH vibration of coordination water molecules [35]. The emission intensity of Eu3+ and the sensitivity and selectivity of nanoprobe to TCs can be enhanced by using auxiliary ligand citrate (Cit) to displace coordinated water molecules [36]. Therefore, the sensing platform for quantitative detection of tetracycline is constructed with carbon dots and Eu3+-luminous center as reference and response signal respectively. In the construction of ratio fluorescence, the emission position of the reference signal needs to be fixed. At present, some studies introduce nitrogen elements in the synthesis of CDs to fix the emission wavelength [37,38], but the methods lead to fluorescence quenching in the state of powder aggregation, and the emission peak position of most CDs shift with the different excitation energy. Therefore, it is really important to establish an efficient method to fix the CDs domain and ensure the stability of its fluorescence and excitation.

Aminoclay, is a kind of synthetic layered clay material with high purity and good dispersion. It can provide soft carriers for rare earth complexes due to the existence of amine function, so it has become one of the commonly used substrates for the preparation of hybrid luminescent nanomaterials [39]. Here, an interesting AC-modified CDs-rare earth contained nanoprobe is constructed to detect trace amount of TC. Specifically, AC is used to encapsulate the limited domain of CDs as a reference signal, and grafts Cit-Eu as an identification unit. With the increase of tetracycline content, the fluorescence intensity of CDs in the prepared ratio probe remain unaffected, while Eu3+ is extremely sensitive to TC, and its characteristic peak intensity gradually increase, in which the reaction system illustrates a fluorescence transition from blue to red. As expected, the probe not only demonstrates high sensitivity and reliability, but also can distinctly display the change signal of fluorescence color in the sensing system, which fully satisfies the requirements of intelligent sensor testing, and provides a valid way for on-site qualitative identification and semi-quantitative detection of TC (Scheme 1 ).

Citric acid (Cit) was purchased from Tianjin Komeo Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Urea was purchased from Tianjin Hongyan Reagent Factory. Magnesium chloride hexahydrate (MgCl2⋅6H2O), polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) and sodium alginate (SA) were purchased from Shanghai Titanchem Co., Ltd. 3-amino propyltriethoxysilane (APTES) and N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) were purchased from China Shanghai Aladdin Industrial Co., Ltd. 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)carbodiimidehy-drochloride (EDC), Europium nitrate hexahydrate (Eu(NO3)3⋅6H2O) and tris(hydroxymethylmetyl)aminomethane (Tris) were from Adamas Reagent Co., Ltd. Hydrochloric acid (HCl) was purchased from Yantai Shuangshuang Chemical Co., Ltd. Tetracycline (TC) were obtain from Shandong Xiya Chemical Industry Co., Ltd. All solutions in the experiment were prepared with deionized water and all the chemicals used were analytical reagents.

The transmission electron microscope images (TEM) were recorded by Tecnai G2 F30 microscope at an accelerating voltage of 300 kV. The powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns data (2θ = 5°∼80°) were measured by Rigaku Dmax 2400 diffractometer. The Fourier transform infrared spectra (FTIR) of materials were characterized using standard KBr disk technique in the range from 4000 to 400 cm−1 by a Brooke V70 Fourier transform infrared spectrometer. The surface composition of the samples was analyzed by Thermo ESCALAB 250Xi X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The content of Eu3+ was determined by inductively coupled plasma atomic emission spectrometry (ICP-AES) with an IRIS Advantage ER/S spectrophotometer. The steady-state emission spectra were obtained by F97 Pro fluorescence spectrometer at 350 nm excitation wavelength. The time-resolved fluorescence spectrum was investigated by Edinburgh FS5 equipped with a cooled R928P photomultiplier tube. The UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded on the EVOLUTION 201 UV–Visible spectrophotometer.

The blue emissive carbon dots were synthesized by hydrothermal method using citric acid as carbon source and urea as nitrogen source and passivator according to the literature method with some modification [40,41]. In detail, 1 g of citric acid and 2 g of urea were fully dissolved in 10 mL deionized water and then transferred to a polytetrafluoroethylene lined stainless steel reactor for 4 h at 160 °C. The resulted CDs were purified by 500DA's dialysis bag for one day and then freeze-dried for further use.

The method of coating CDs was similar to that of titanium dioxide reported in the literature [42]: A total amount of 1.68 g MgCl2⋅6H2O (8.26 mmol) and 1.23 g APTES (5.56 mmol) were dissolved in 50 mL anhydrous ethanol, the mixture was then stirred at room temperature for 12 h. Then 10 mL ethanol solution containing 20 mg CDs and 1.23 g APTES was mixed to stir for 5 h. The precipitation was collected and purified with ethanol for 3 times to remove the impure matter. Finally, the blue fluorescence composite material was obtained.

Firstly, a total amount of 25 mg citric acid, 12 mg NHS and 10 mg of EDC were mixed in 30 mL DMF/ethanol = 1/1 (v/v) solution and stirred for 4 h. Secondly, 200 mg of CDs@AC was added to the above solution and continue to stir for 24 h at 50 °C. When the amino group on AC and the carboxyl group on Cit reacted completely, the obtained precipitates were washed with ethanol and redispersed in 15 mL ethanol, then Eu(NO3)3 ·6H2O (5 mL, 20 mg) ethanol solution was added and reacted in oil bath at 50 °C for 6 h. The final products were collected and depurated with ethanol to remove excess unreacted lanthanum salts.

200 μL of CDs@AC-Cit-Eu nanoprobe aqueous solution (1 mg/mL) was added to Tris-HCl buffer (pH = 9) solution to form 2 mL suspension. The response of the composite to TCs was tested by titration, and the steady-state fluorescence emission spectrum was recorded under 350 nm excitation light. The same operation was also carried out for the detection of TCs in actual samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Calculation of TC in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: Pure milk was treated refers to the previous report [43,44]: 300 g/L of trichloroacetic acid was added to the milk at a volume ratio of 1 %. After ultrasonic treatment for 20 min, biological impurities were removed. The obtained supernatant was further treated with a filter membrane (0.22 μm), and then adjusted to pH = 9 with a small amount of NaOH (6 M). The honey was taken from natural wild honey, diluted 20 times with buffer solution, and the solid particles were removed with a filter membrane (0.22 μm). Lake water and tap water were obtained locally and the impurities were filtered with a 0.22 μm membrane. The obtained filtrate was formulated into TC standard solutions of different concentrations. The fluorescence intensity ratio was determined after the probe was added, and the concentration and recovery of TC were calculated according to the fitted working curve. Each sample was tested 3 times and then the relative standard deviation (RSD) was calculated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of TC visualization test paper
ce:section.ce:para: The successful fixation of the CDs@AC-Cit-Eu nanoprobe on the test paper is a crucial step before TC visual detection. Firstly, the ordinary filter paper was cut into small discs with a diameter of about 7 mm, and then impregnated in the dispersion solution of CDs@AC-Cit-Eu nanoprobe (1 mg/mL) for incubation for 1 h. After the filter paper was taken out and dried naturally, the test paper was obtained for the detection of TC. TC solution of different concentrations were dropped on the prepared test paper, the fluorescence color changes of the test paper under the irradiation of 365 nm UV lamp with naked eyes were observed. The relationship between the color RGB value and the concentration of tetracycline was calculated with the color capture and processing program in smart phone.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CDs@AC-Cit-Eu luminescent film and its response to TC
ce:section.ce:para: SA/PVA/CDs@AC-Cit-Eu nanocomposite membrane material was prepared with polyvinyl alcohol and sodium alginate as main skeleton materials, while CaCl2 was used as crosslinking agent [45]. A total amount of 600 mg SA was fully dissolved in 30 mL of distilled water at room temperature to obtain a 2 % transparent solution, and then 500 mg of PVA was dissolved in 8 mL of distilled water at 95 °C to prepare a 5 % PVA solution. 5 mL PVA solution was mixed with 28 mL SA solution, and 1.0 g glycerol was added as plasticizer. The mixture was continued to stir at room temperature for 1 h. 50 mg CDs@AC-Cit-Eu powder was dispersed in 3 mL distilled water, and then added to the above SA/PVA mixed solution after ultrasonic treatment for 20 min. After stirring the mixture for 1 h, the uniform water suspension obtained was poured on the glass plate. After the film was formed, it was immersed in 0.1 % CaCl2 solution for cross-linking for 4 h to enhance the toughness and hydrophobicity of the film. The residual CaCl2 was rinsed with distilled water to get the luminescent film. In the response test, the membrane was soaked in aqueous solutions with different concentrations of TC for 2 min and then dried naturally. The membrane was fixed on the swim bladder with glue and placed in a simulated water flow environment for TC detection.

The morphology of the CDs and CDs@AC-Cit-Eu were characterized by TEM. For CDs, a uniformly distributed spherical shape with a particle size of about 2 nm was observed (Fig. 1 a). Aminoclay is a kind of organic magnesium silicate clay with special lamellar structure, and the schematic diagram is shown in Fig. 1b. The morphology of CDs@AC-Cit-Eu indicates that CDs are uniformly distributed on irregular flake aminoclay (Fig. 1c). As shown in Fig. 1d, the EDS measurements display that in addition to C, N, O, Mg, Cl and Si, CDs@AC-Cit-Eu samples also contained Eu elements, manifesting that the formation of CDs@AC-Cit-Eu nanocomposites is triumphant. The content of grafted Eu is quantitative determined to be 2.13 % through the inductively coupled plasma atomic emission spectrometry (ICP).

The microstructure of CDs, AC, CDs@AC and CDs@AC-Cit-Eu were further confirmed by XRD analysis. As exhibited in Fig. S1a, CDs have a large inclusion peak of CDs near 2θ = 25°, which overlap with the diffraction peak of aminoclay at 21.49° to a certain extent. Since the coating amounts of CDs are relatively small, the diffraction peaks of CDs@AC are almost the same as that of pure AC. The diffraction peak did not change significantly after grafting the Cit-Eu complex, indicating that the composite is prepared successfully. FTIR was used to characterize the surface groups and structures of CDs@AC-Cit-Eu, which are shown in Fig. S1b. The absorption peaks at 1131 and 1022 cm−1 are originated from the Si-C and Si-O-Si bonds of typical aminoclay structures [46]. The strong absorption peaks at 1400 and 1631 cm−1 are ascribed to the vibration of CO-NH/CO and O-H [47]. Before and after grafting Cit, the peak intensity at 1400 cm−1 decrease first and then increase, which is due to the small amount of CDs containing amide bonds and the increase of carboxyl group. The peak intensity at 1500 cm−1 first appears and then weakens due to the decrease of -NH2 after grafting Cit. The absorption peak of CDs@AC-Cit-Eu coordination with Eu3+ shifts to 1387 cm−1, which is due to the vibration of NO3 − during the grafting process of Cit-Eu [48], indicating that the Cit-Eu complex is successfully modified on the surface of CDs@AC. In order to further prove that the CDs was loaded, the UV–vis absorption spectra of CDs, AC and CDs@AC were measured. As shown in Fig. S3, AC has no absorption peak, but there is an absorption peak at 348 nm after loading CDs, which also confirm the successful modification of AC by CDs.

The elemental composition and functional groups of CDs@AC-Cit-Eu were determined by XPS. Fig. S2a shows the XPS full-scan spectrum of CDs@AC-Cit-Eu and the surface chemical composition of the nanocomposite is studied in detail. The peaks at the binding energies of 102.15, 285.86, 402.40, 532.21 and 1305.41eV are corresponded to Si 2p, C 1s, N 1s, O 1s and Mg 1s, respectively. The representative peaks at 284.8, 285.2, 286.2 and 288.3 eV in C 1s (Fig. S2b) high resolution X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy further confirm the existence of C-C/C-H, C-N, C-O and CO [49,50] bonds in CDs@AC-Cit-Eu. The characteristic peaks at 399.4, 401.5 and 406.8 eV in the energy spectrum of N 1s (Fig. S2c) are corresponded to N-C, N-H and N-O bonds respectively [51,52], which reinforce the successful of CDs@AC-Cit-Eu synthesis. For Eu 3d, due to the multiple interactions of spin orbits, the energy spectrum splits out four strong peaks (Fig. S2d). The energy spectrum peaks of Eu3+ 3d3/2, Eu2+ 3d3/2, Eu3+ 3d5/2 and Eu2+ 5d3/2 are 1164.51, 1155.54, 1134.82 and 112,454 eV, respectively [24,53,54], while the energy difference between the two peaks of Eu3+ 3d is 29.689 eV, which is in good agreement with the coordination energy difference between Eu3+ and O atoms reported in the literature [55,56]. This provides strong evidence for the successful synthesis of CDs@AC-Cit-Eu nanomaterials. The results of FTIR and XPS exhibit that there are -NH2 and -COOH groups on the surface of CDs@AC-Cit-Eu, and the magnificent water dispersion of the probe is enhanced by these hydrophilic functional groups.

The corresponding fluorescence tests of CDs were analyzed since it can emit intense blue fluorescence by itself. As shown in Fig. S4a, the best excitation and emission of CDs are located at 350 nm and 443 nm respectively. Under different excitation conditions, the emission position of CDs is unchanged (Fig. S4b), indicating that the fluorescence emission peak of CDs is not affected by the change of excitation wavelength. At the same time, the response of CDs to TC was also studied (Fig. S4c). With the increase of TC concentration, the fluorescence intensity of CDs decrease slightly, but the response of fluorescence intensity of CDs@AC-Cit-Eu composite nanoprobe to TC at 443 nm is negligible. In order to verify that the emission peak of the nanoprobe at 443 nm indeed originates from CDs, the fluorescence emission spectra of CDs, AC and CDs@AC-Cit-Eu were further compared, and the results are consistent with expectations (Fig. S4d).

The possible mechanism of the fluorescence response of the nanoprobe in the presence and absence of TC were further discussed and confirmed. In the absence of TC, CDs@AC-Cit-Eu only display the emission peak of CDs at 443 nm, while the fluorescence of Eu3+ is still turned off, which is because the coordination of europium ion is unsaturated and the coordination water molecules have non-radiation quenching effect. After the addition of 5 μM TC, the emission intensity of CDs is not affected, while the transition of Eu3+ ions from the ground state (5D0) to the excited state (7FJ, J = 1,2,3,4) causes a significant enhancement of the characteristic peaks at 593, 617, 652 and 695 nm, among which the hypersensitive transition of 5D0→7F2 is the most prominent (Fig. 2 a and b). This phenomenon may be caused by the following reasons:
(і)
the triplet excited energy level of TC with β-diketone structure is about 1860 cm−1 higher than the 5D0 energy level of Eu3+ [57], enabling the effective energy transformation from TC molecules to Eu3+. As illustrated in Fig. 2c, the excited electrons first transit from the ground state (S0) to the first excited singlet state (S1, 36200 cm−1), and then to the lower energy triplet state (T1, 19160 cm−1) [41]. The triplet energy can be transferred directly to the lowest excited state of Eu3+ ion (5D0, 17300 cm−1). When the electron returns from the excited state to the ground state, it will emit red fluorescence (among which the 5D0 transition at 616 nm is the most sensitive).
(іі)
The coordination ability between TC and europium ion is much greater than that between water and europium ion, which reduces the non-radiative quenching caused by high frequency oscillation of OH [24,58,59]. The number of water molecules substituted by TC after coordination is calculated by fluorescence lifetime decay test (Fig. 2d). The time-dependent fluorescence spectra of Eu3+ excited at 350 nm are analyzed by using the corresponding bi-exponential function in OriginPro9, and the average decay lifetime in H2O and D2O buffer is calculated by using formula (1) and (2) [60]. The coordination number of “H2O” in the system is calculated using Eq. (3) established by Horrocks and Supkowski [58,61].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of the optimal detection conditions for TC
ce:section.ce:para: In order to ensure the best working conditions of the CDs@AC-Cit-Eu nanoprobe, the excitation wavelength, buffer system, pH values, reaction time and stability were optimized to obtain the optimum experimental results. As shown in Fig. S5, CDs@AC-Cit-Eu can achieve the most effective fluorescence emission after adding TC at the excitation wavelength of 350 nm, which is consistent with the optimum excitation of CDs itself, and the optimal excitation wavelength is 350 nm. Subsequently, the impact of buffering system and pH on fluorescence intensity ratio (I617/I443) were investigated. As can be seen from Fig. S6a and S6b, I617/I443 ratio is the maximum in Tris-HCl buffer system and increases gradually within pH 3.0–9.0, while I617/I443 ratio decreases promptly at higher pH (pH > 9.0). This is on account of the energy cannot be transferred to Eu3+ after protonation of TC under acidic conditions, while Eu3+ will partially form europium hydroxide precipitation under strong alkaline conditions, resulting in a decrease in fluorescence intensity ratio [34]. Hence, the Tris-HCl solution with pH = 9 is chosen as the best buffer system for the determination of TC. The I617/I443 ratio of CDs@AC-Cit-Eu nanoprobe at different time after adding 5 μM TC was also measured. As shown in Fig. S6c, the fluorescence response could be completed quickly and reached equilibrium within 30 s, and remained basically stable. Therefore, 30 s is selected as the best response time. In addition, the stability of CDs@AC-Cit-Eu under high pressure illumination of Xenon lamp within 800 s was also investigated. Normalized results displayed in Fig. S6d shows the synthesized nanoprobe has good photostability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity and selectivity of TC test of CDs@AC-Cit-Eu
ce:section.ce:para: The sensitivity and examination range of this nanoprobe for tetracycline testing were evaluated under the optimal conditions. As illustrated in Fig. 3 a, there is no change in fluorescence intensity of the fluorescent nanoprobe at 443 nm, but the relationship between the emission intensity and TC (from 0.05 to 25 μM) at 617 nm indicates a gradually increasing trend. As can be seen from Fig. 3b, there is a satisfactory linear relationship between the dosage of TC and the fluorescence intensity ratio (I617/I443) over the range of 0.05–12.5 μM and 10–25 μM, with a correlation coefficient of 0.99483 and 0.99125. The limit of detection (LOD) and quantification (LOQ) are calculated as low as 3.133 nM and 0.05 μM, respectively, which the LOD is far below the maximum residue limit of 0.676 μM and 0.225 μM for TC in milk stipulated by the Food and Drug Administration (FDA) of the United States and the European Union [62,63]. In addition, the sensing performance are compared with majority methods antecedently reported (Table S2). The CIE chromaticity coordinates of CDs@AC-Cit-Eu with the increase of TC concentration are calculated according to their emission spectrum (Table S3). As shown in Fig. 3c, the CDs@AC-Cit-Eu nanoprobe presents a remarkable adjustable fluorescence evolution from blue (0.1894, 0.1489) to red (0.4846, 0.2695) with the increasing content of tetracycline. And the color change can be easily discernible with the naked eye under the irradiation of 365 nm UV lamp (Fig. 3d), which makes the detection of tetracycline visual and effortless. More importantly, this multi-color fluorescence strategy can reduce the interference caused by detection conditions or hardware factors, and also provide a prerequisite for the development of portable TC vision sensor.

There are often many other metal ions and small molecules in real samples in the environment, and the existence of these impurities may affect the sensitivity and accuracy of the detection. To evaluate the feasibility of the nanoprobe in actual application, the anti-interference and selectivity of the nanoprobe to TC detection under the existence of K+, Na+, Ca2+, Cu2+, Hg2+, Mg2+, Pb2+, Zn2+, l-aspartic acid (Asp), l-glutamic acid (Glu), glycine (Gly), cysteine (Cys), glutathione (GSH) and glucose were further investigated, which are common in real samples. As illustrated in Fig. 4 a, the existence of the above impurities have no effect on the fluorescence signal of the TC response of the nanoprobe, indicating the excellent selectively of CDs@AC-Cit-Eu nanoprobe to TC. Simultaneously, the fluorescence response of the probe to other antibiotics such as thiamphenicol, chloramphenicol and kanamycin sulfate were also investigated. The results in Fig. 4b indicates that these substances have less influence on the fluorescence intensity ratio of the probe, which adequately testified that CDs@AC-Cit-Eu has a high selective recognition ability to TC. Furthermore, the illustrations in Fig. 4b and c exhibit that TC can trigger sensitive color changes in the fluorescence sensing system in the presence of certain potential coexisting substances. This mighty convinced that this proportional fluorescence switching method has excellent selectivity for TC detection and provides an effective method for qualitative identification of TC.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of TC detection in actual food and environmental water samples
ce:section.ce:para: The application of CDs@AC-Cit-Eu probe to the analysis of TC in milk, honey, tap water and lake water samples can not only evaluate the practicability of the method, but also have a certain guiding significance for the assessment of dietary health and environmental pollution. Table S4 reveals that the recovery is as high as 95 %∼112 %, and the relative standard deviation is less than 6 %, indicating that the nanoprobe has relatively stable precision and reliability for the determination of TC in real samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Visual test strip and smartphone-assisted detection platform for semi-quantitative TC analysis
ce:section.ce:para: In view of the high sensitivity and selective recognition of TC by CDs@AC-Cit-Eu ratio type nano-fluorescent probe, a paper-based sensor was designed to realize the rapid visual sensing of TC by changing the fluorescence color of filter paper. After coating CDs@AC-Cit-Eu nanoprobe on the filter paper and irradiating with a 365 nm UV lamp, it presents blue fluorescence. As shown in Fig. 5 , the color of the test paper gradually changes from blue to red when different concentrations of TC solution are added to the test paper. The fluorescence color change can be easily observed under UV lamp, which realizes the qualitative analysis of tetracycline.

With the development of science and technology, smart phone as a convenient platform for communication and entertainment have entered thousands of households. In recent years, scientists have designed a variety of rapid detection platforms by using the voice control system of smart phones themselves, high-definition cameras with adjustable parameters and temperature sensors [64–67]. In order to realize the quantitative analysis of tetracycline in the sample to be tested, we introduced the color scanning application (APP) in the smart phone as the fluorescence color reader and analyzer, and carried out digital semi-quantitative analysis of the fluorescence color (RGB) of the test paper with the change of the concentration of the substance to be tested. The combination of the test paper and mobile phone not only saves the detection cost, but also makes the results accurate and reliable. It realizes the convenient application of smart phone in daily life, and has important significance to promote the development and construction of smart phone detection device.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Rapid visual detection of TC in flowing water environment by vertical float with luminescent thin film
ce:section.ce:para: In order to further quickly and real-time monitor the concentration level of tetracycline in the actual water environment, we put forward the following idea: (1) to integrate the nanoprobe into the thin film device and bind it to the vertical float commonly used in fishing; (2) with the advantage that vertical float in the actual mobile water samples is conducive to floating and recovery, the real-time and fast testing of TC in dynamic water environment can be achieved. Specifically, the CDs@AC-Cit-Eu nanoprobe is dispersed in the mixed solution of SA/PVA, poured on a glass plate, dried and cross-linked with CaCl2 to obtain a kind of luminescent film. The films are then impregnated in a series of standard concentrations of tetracycline solutions (0, 2.5 μM and 10 μM) for 2 min. After drying, the films are fixed in the form of a “sandwich structure"on the 1,3,5 position of the vertical float as a standard colorimetric card (as shown on the upper left of Fig. 6 ). Some blank luminous films are attached to the 2 and 4 positions of the vertical float and connected in series with the above standard colorimetric card to simulate the dynamic monitoring of TC in the actual water environment. There is no change in the fluorescent color of the film on the vertical float before and after it is placed in the flowing water environment (as shown on the upper right of Fig. 6). However, with the continuous increase of TC concentration, the fluorescence of the blank sample gradually turns red and the color change is obvious. It can be determined from Fig. 6 and Movie S1 in supporting information that the concentration of TC detected in the blank sample is between 0–2.5 μM and 2.5–10 μM, which realizes the qualitative and semi-quantitative real-time monitoring of TC concentration in the flowing water environment.
Supplementary data related to this article can be found at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jlumin.2021.118497.

Multimedia component 2 Multimedia component 2

ce:section.ce:section-title: Establishment of logic gate circuit of CDs@AC-Cit-Eu nanoprobe in TC detection
ce:section.ce:para: Considering the sensitivity of CDs@AC-Cit-Eu fluorescence nanoprobe to TC trigger, an advanced analysis device based on one-to-two logic gate was constructed by taking TC concentration as input signal and two fluorescence emission signals as output signal [68]. As indicated in Fig. 7 a, combined with the actual detection results, the TC concentration is used as the input, and the existence and non-existence of TC are defined as “1” and “0”, respectively. The normalized fluorescence intensity signals of FBlue (443 nm) and FRed (617 nm) are used as double outputs, and the normalized fluorescence intensity corresponding to 2.5 μM dose TC is selected as the threshold (Fig. 7b). Without TC, the intensity of FBlue does not change, and the intensity FRed does not increase, resulting in output (1,0). When TC is present, FBlue remains unchanged, while FRed increases, and output (0,1) is generated. Three output combinations NOT (0,1), YES (1,0), PASS 0 (0,0) for semi-quantitative assessment of tetracycline concentrations. As shown in Fig. 7c and d, if the TC content is less than 2.5 μM, the emission signal of the detection system is mainly blue fluorescence, resulting in output (1,0). However, when the content of tetracycline is greater than 2.5 μM, the red fluorescence signal of the detection system is dominant and the output is produced (0,1). And when the concentration of TC is 2.5 μM, the signal intensity of blue fluorescence is almost the same as that of red fluorescence, and the output is (0,0). Each concentration has a standard color, according to the color of the digital photos taken, the different concentrations of TC can be clearly displayed, such as blue (0–2.5 μM), purple (2.5–10 μM) and red (10–25 μM).

To sum up, a highly sensitive, wide range and reliable ratio fluorescence sensor based on aminoclay coated CDs and rare earth doping has been designed to realize the fast visual detection of TC. The detection limit is as low as 3.133 nM, in the range of 25 μM TC, which realizes the accurate detection of trace TC and meet the requirements of control and detection of TC in food and environmental water. The sensitive response of the nanoprobe to TC concentration can not only realize visual analysis sensing through the chromaticity analysis application of smart phone, but also design a one-to-two logic gate switch, which provides the possibility for the application of nanoprobe in intelligent device. More importantly, in order to further quickly and timely monitor the concentration level of tetracycline in the actual water environment, after the nanoprobe is integrated into the thin film device and bound to the vertical float commonly used in fishing, the qualitative and semi-quantitative real-time monitoring of tetracycline concentration in mobile water environment can be realized.
